title: Vector Magnonics: Electrical Injection and Control of Spin Flow in Altermagnets
authors: Yanmeng Lei, Rui-Chun Xiao, Weiwei Lin, Tao Yu
abstract: Altermagnets host chirally split magnons that promise unique functionalities for information processing. However, their distinctive transport signatures, crucial for experimental identification and manipulation, remain elusive. Here, we predict that a spin accumulation electrically injects a ``vector" or multidirectional magnon spin current into an altermagnet, comprising both longitudinal and sizable transverse components. Notably, this transverse current exhibits a sign reversal away from the source and can be switched on or off by reorienting the Néel vector. While such a transverse current is found to be not forbidden even in conventional antiferromagnets, we demonstrate through quantum-kinetic calculations that in altermagnets, the transverse response is enhanced by two orders of magnitude due to broken parity-time symmetry. This giant enhancement provides a decisive transport fingerprint for detecting magnon spin splitting and Néel-vector orientation, offering a clear criterion to experimentally distinguish altermagnets from conventional antiferromagnets.
summary: This paper predicts that altermagnets can support a multidirectional 'vector' magnon spin current when electrically injected. It demonstrates that the transverse component of this current is significantly larger in altermagnets than in antiferromagnets, providing a clear experimental fingerprint to identify altermagnetic materials.
main_problem: The study aims to identify and manipulate distinctive transport signatures of altermagnets (ATMs) for information processing, specifically focusing on how to experimentally distinguish them from conventional antiferromagnets.
main_result: The authors predict a 'vector' magnon spin current with both longitudinal and sizable transverse components, where the transverse response is enhanced by two orders of magnitude in altermagnets due to broken parity-time symmetry.
method: The researchers use microscopic quantum kinetic equations to model electrical spin injection, diffusion, and dephasing, combined with a symmetry-based expansion of the response tensor.
model_or_system: A heavy-metal/altermagnet (NM/ATM) interface modeled using a minimal square-lattice two-sublattice Hamiltonian including inter-sublattice exchange, anisotropy, and an applied magnetic field.
key_observables: Longitudinal and transverse magnon spin current components, and the transverse-to-longitudinal spin-current ratio.
important_parameters: Exchange coupling J1 = 1 meV, spin S = 3/2, lattice constant a = 1 nm, and relaxation time tau_m = 1 ns.
main_assumptions: The model assumes a relaxation time tau_m that is equal for both magnon modes and focuses on the diffusion-driven transport regime.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a schematic of diffusive magnon transport and a comparison of flow in AFMs vs ATMs; Figure 2 illustrates injected magnon populations in the Brillouin zone; Figure 3 shows the dependence of longitudinal and transverse spin currents on the Neel vector orientation.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of altermagnet transport, describes the microscopic model and quantum kinetic framework, presents the symmetry analysis of the response tensor, details the results regarding vector spin currents and their enhancement, and concludes with the experimental implications for distinguishing altermagnets.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Diffusive magnon spin transport in ATMs vs AFMs under electrical injection. (a) A charge current Iˆx in the nor- mal metal (NM) creates a spin accumulation ∆µ ∥ˆz via spin Hall effect, driving a vector magnon flow in ATMs. Red and blue arrows indicate the reversed transverse flow. (b) Minimal ATMs model with the N´eel vector n forming an angle θ to the diffusion ˆz-axis. (c,d) Transverse magnon flow in AFMs and ATMs when n ∦ˆz. Red (blue) denotes positive (negative) transverse group velocities v⊥∥ˆx of injected magnons.
FIG. 2. Injected population of modes-1 and 2 magnons under different N´eel-vector directions θ = {0, π/3}.

FIG. 3. Dependence of longitudinal Jz,x ∥ [(a,b)] and transverse Jz,x ⊥ [(c,d)] magnon spin currents on the N´eel-vector direction θ at z ≳0. (e) and (f) illustrate efficient control of these spin currents by the applied magnetic field at θ = π/6.

FIG. 4. (a) Transverse spin current Jz ⊥as a function of the propagation distance z for different orientations θ of the N´eel- vector. (b) Spatial profiles of the mode-resolved contributions Jz ⊥(v⊥ 1 ) and Jz ⊥(v⊥ 1 ) at θ = π/6.
